strengths: Clearly identifies three specific real-world objects with provenance and authentic primary sources
Explicitly states and repeatedly anchors commentary to the chosen IA prompt about personal experience and knowledge
Uses relevant TOK concepts (e.g. experiential vs propositional knowledge, metaphor as a way of knowing) to justify object inclusion
Draws on detailed examples from Nagel, Feynman and Fischer to support claims and illustrate different aspects of knowledge
weaknesses: At times the analysis drifts into descriptive or speculative exposition rather than sustained, focused engagement with the prompt
Repetition of ideas and overlap between object justifications reduces overall precision and coherence
Real-world contexts (such as the significance of specific editions) are only briefly sketched and lack depth
Key assertions (e.g. identity of experiential knowledge with personal experience) are under-developed and unsupported by counterarguments or additional evidence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs exhibition demonstrates solid achievement in the 5â6 band: three well-chosen objects, clear links to the IA prompt, and generally coherent justification grounded in TOK terminology. However, repetition, occasional lack of critical depth, and imprecise contextual details prevent the work from reaching the 7â8 band. Awarding 6 marks accurately reflects the strengths in object selection and conceptual grounding balanced against the limitations in analytical focus and evidential integration.

annotations.comment: Including the image of Nagelâs article helps ground the discussion in a tangible primary source, reinforcing the authenticity of the real-world object.
There is noticeable repetition between paragraphs; condensing overlapping ideas would enhance precision and clarity.
Consider integrating discussion of propositional versus experiential knowledge frameworks to deepen analytical rigor.
The third object is clearly identified with author and date, setting a solid contextual basis for analysis of procedural knowledge.
The distinction between content-based and procedural knowledge aligns well with TOK concepts, demonstrating clear understanding of ways of knowing.
The student clearly states the IA prompt, anchoring the exhibitionâs focus and ensuring that each object is evaluated in relation to the question of personal experience and knowledge.
The heading for Object 2 succinctly identifies the source and its authors, providing a clear starting point for its contextual analysis.
The image of the cover is included but its relevance to the argument about personal experience in learning physics is not explicitly connected in the text.
Repetition of phrases such as âpersonal experience assistsâ could be reduced to improve focus and avoid redundancy.
This paragraph predominantly summarizes Nagelâs argument rather than critically engaging with implications for the relationship between experiential and propositional knowledge.
The claim that imagination fails to capture bat experience is asserted without reference to alternative philosophical positions or empirical studies on imaginationâs limits.
Stating that experiential knowledge and personal experience are identical is a bold assertion that would benefit from engagement with counterarguments or secondary literature.
Describing Fischer as âarguably the best chess playerâ is a subjective claim; support it with historical ranking data or credible sources.
Equating personal experience directly with procedural knowledge oversimplifies their relationship; engage with theories of embodied cognition or skill acquisition.
The closing justification effectively contrasts single-instance content learning with cumulative experiential knowledge, tying back to the central prompt.
The suggestion that metaphor âhaltsâ acquisition of exact objective knowledge is speculative; consider discussing cognitive scaffolding research to balance this claim.
The description of Object 1 lacks specific provenance or edition details, limiting the real-world context that would strengthen the objectâs authenticity.
The justification clearly links Nagelâs article to the prompt by arguing that personal experience is sufficient for acquiring experiential knowledge.
The explanation of how Feynmanâs lectures use personal experience could be strengthened by citing a direct quote or specific lecture example.
Identifying the electron-as-water metaphor effectively illustrates how personal experience can scaffold understanding of abstract concepts.
Include precise lecture or page references to strengthen the academic rigor of the examples provided.
The final paragraph acknowledges the limitations of relying on personal experience, demonstrating critical self-awareness in the justification.
The robot analogy is informal and detracts from scholarly tone; consider removing or rephrasing to maintain analytical focus.
Consider synthesising insights across all three objects to draw out overarching conclusions rather than treating each in isolation.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: The exhibition does not reach the standard described by the other levels or does not use one of the IA prompts provided.

The exhibition presents
three objects, but the
real-world contexts of
these objects are not
stated, or the images
presented may be
highly generic images of
types of object rather
than of specific
real-world objects. Links
between the objects
and the selected IA
prompt are made, but
these are minimal,
tenuous, or it is not
clear what the student is
trying to convey.
There is very little
justification offered for
the inclusion of each
object in the exhibition.
The commentary on the
objects is highly
descriptive or consists
only of unsupported
assertions.

The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of the objects may be implied rather than explicitly stated. Basic links between the objects and the selected IA prompt are made, but the explanation of these links is unconvincing and/or unfocused. There is a superficial justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Reasons for the inclusion of the objects are offered, but these are not supported by appropriate evidence and/or lack relevance to the selected IA prompt. There may be significant repetition across the justifications of the different objects

The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of these objects may be vaguely or imprecisely stated. There is some explanation of the links between the three objects and the selected IA prompt. There is some justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Some of the points are supported by evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

The exhibition identifies three objects and their real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are explained, although this explanation may lack precision and clarity in parts. There is a justification of the contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. Many of the points are supported by appropriate evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

The exhibition clearly identifies three objects and their specific real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are clearly made and well-explained. There is a strong justification of the particular contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. All, or nearly all, of the points are well-supported by appropriate evidence and explicit references to the selected IA prompt.

rubricCriterionStrands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
